item.title: [SCMP] Why Singapore PM Wong moved swiftly on Faishal Ibrahim’s resignation
item.description: Singapore’s acting minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs Muhammad Faishal Ibrahim resigned about a month after the prime minister learned of his improper interactions with a woman, a move observers say shows Lawrence Wong’s swift handling of the first scandal under his watch to contain any political fallout. They told This Week in Asia that while there was no affair in Faishal’s case, it made sense for the party to deal with the consequences quickly in case of leaks as the woman was a member of t
item.content:encoded:
Singapore’s acting minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs Muhammad Faishal Ibrahim resigned about a month after the prime minister learned of his improper interactions with a woman, a move observers say shows Lawrence Wong’s swift handling of the first scandal under his watch to contain any political fallout.

They told This Week in Asia that while there was no affair in Faishal’s case, it made sense for the party to deal with the consequences quickly in case of leaks as the woman was a member of the public, not a party member whom the ruling People’s Action Party (PAP) could counsel or discipline.

Furthermore, Faishal’s position as acting minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs meant Wong had to be sensitive to the community’s reaction – and potential embarrassment – if the matter was not swiftly extinguished and a replacement sought for the post.

On Monday, Faishal resigned from the cabinet and parliament after inappropriate interactions he had with a woman that resulted in harassment accusations by both parties became known to the prime minister.

No criminal action was found but Faishal, 58, who was also senior minister of state for home affairs, reflected and accepted that his conduct fell short of the standards “expected of a political office holder and MP”, Wong said in a statement on Monday.

Wong revealed that most of their interactions took place through online messages and they also met on the sidelines of public events. Both parties later made allegations of harassment against each other, and the matter was referred to police.

Faishal, who is married with two children, wrote in his resignation letter from government and his party: “While there was no physical relationship between us, and I did not intend for the interactions to develop into one, there was a lapse of judgment on my part in the way I handled the interactions, and in failing to establish clearer boundaries at an earlier stage.”

Faishal’s case inevitably drew comparisons with another explosive revelation in July 2023, when former speaker of parliament Tan Chuan-Jin and MP Cheng Li Hui, both from the PAP, resigned from their positions after being in an extramarital affair.

Political observers noted the much shorter timeline in Wong’s handling of Faishal’s resignation compared with the affair between Tan and Cheng, which then prime minister Lee Hsien Loong found out about after the 2020 general election, at least two years before they stepped down in 2023.

Lee said in a press conference in July 2023 that as recently as February that year, he had counselled the lawmakers involved to end it. Tan offered his resignation then, but Lee said he told him to ensure his constituents were taken care of first and that the affair had to end.

Lee added then that he had “very recently” come across information strongly suggesting the pair’s relationship had continued.

Teo Kay Key, senior research fellow at the Institute of Policy Studies, noted that the full details of Faishal’s interactions were unknown, making comparisons difficult. However, the difference in timelines was “quite clear”, perhaps reflecting lessons learned from the public reaction to Tan’s affair, she said.

“Of course, given that the woman had sent details to the PMO [Prime Minister’s Office] and also that both parties had actually made harassment allegations, there is a possibility that the news would have gotten out from other channels and that it would have been more expedient to deal with the whole matter swiftly,” Teo said.

While the details of the relationship were scant and what actually happened was unclear, Teo added that this incident, taken together with the previous resignations, revealed the PAP’s expectations for how its MPs and political office holders should behave in their personal relationships and how this would affect their political careers.

Independent political analyst Felix Tan raised concern that the lack of details could lead to greater public speculation online.

He said Wong was not being stricter than his predecessor but wanted to stem the damage early instead of letting it “evolve into something greater that he has to deal with in the future”.

“One possible question that may arise is whether Faishal’s decision to step down was intended to bring the matter to a close, particularly if there was concern that it could become public or be pursued further. While there is no evidence to support such a conclusion, it is the kind of question that may emerge in the absence of greater clarity,” Felix Tan said.

He also noted that Leader of the House Indranee Rajah had earlier this year stressed integrity and honesty regarding Workers’ Party chief Pritam Singh’s conviction for lying to a parliamentary committee, and this was a reflection of how the PAP believed parliamentarians should conduct themselves.

Former nominated MP (NMP) Calvin Cheng wrote on social media that Faishal should not resign since he did not have an affair. “One has to balance losing a good minister/MP with small failings that all human beings have,” Cheng said.

Walid Jumblatt Abdullah, an associate professor of public policy at Nanyang Technological University, said on social media that given what was known about the interactions, he did not believe Faishal had to resign and that impossible standards were being set that would deter young people from politics.

“The criteria I would use is: is Singapore better off today than yesterday? I think the answer is no. He was a good minister for the country,” Walid said.

Yet Zulkifli Baharudin, a former NMP, told This Week in Asia that the office of Minister-in-charge of Muslim Affairs had to be held to a higher standard.

“The institution of the Muslim minister is a very peculiar one,” he said. “It’s one that’s based on preserving the dignity and the position of Malay Muslims in Singapore and Lawrence Wong must treat the institution of Muslim minister very sensitively so even a minor indiscretion will not be tolerated.”

While some might not consider Faishal’s actions a big or serious deal, Zulkifli noted that the prime minister was taking an uncompromising and high standard expected of the minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs, which would strengthen the institution in the long run.

“It is a tragedy and the community is embarrassed and upset. But one, this is human failure and two, at the end of the day, this will strengthen the institution. This is not just another ministry, it reflects the standing and position of Malay Muslims in Singapore,” Zulkifli said.

Faishal, who has a PhD in management science, entered politics in 2006 in the then Marine Parade group representation constituency following an uncontested walkover.

He was appointed minister of state for national development and home affairs in 2020 and replaced Masagos Zulkifli as acting minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs following the 2025 election, when he was also co-opted into the central executive committee of the ruling PAP.

item.title: [SCMP] Can Europe win a trade war with China? It may depend on how victory is defined
item.description: As trade tensions with China reach boiling point, EU officials are ramping up engagement with Beijing in search of an off-ramp. Quietly behind the scenes, though, Europe is preparing for a trade war. In Brussels, a steely determination has set in to ensure the bloc’s manufacturing heartlands are not deindustrialised by the “China Shock 2.0”. With some industries already haemorrhaging jobs, the EU’s resilience-building and de-risking plans are now laced with deterrents as Brussels looks to acquir
item.content:encoded:
As trade tensions with China reach boiling point, EU officials are ramping up engagement with Beijing in search of an off-ramp.

Quietly behind the scenes, though, Europe is preparing for a trade war. In Brussels, a steely determination has set in to ensure the bloc’s manufacturing heartlands are not deindustrialised by the “China Shock 2.0”.

With some industries already haemorrhaging jobs, the EU’s resilience-building and de-risking plans are now laced with deterrents as Brussels looks to acquire leverage and credible retaliatory tools that would allow it to respond if China uses trade coercively.

Some analysts say the sides are already in a trade war, others think the two sides are still preparing for the inevitable storm – and some say that a trade war should be avoided at all costs.

But a question that is increasingly being asked is: could Europe actually win?

Few in Brussels share the hubris of US President Donald Trump, who in March 2018 said “trade wars are good, and easy to win”.

During a parliamentary hearing last week, the EU’s top trade enforcer, Denis Redonnet, said the purpose of engaging with Beijing was “to avoid sleepwalking at two levels”.

“One, not to sleepwalk into excessive dependencies any more but two, not sleepwalking into an unnecessary trade war with China. It’s going to be a combination of … navigating between these two extremes,” Redonnet said.

Beijing has spent the past eight years building an arsenal of weapons that one senior EU official described recently as “formidable”.

Yet one view is that Europe, if it wishes to hold on to its industry, has little choice but to get into the trenches.

“Something we really try to emphasise in the book is that Europe’s already in the trade war, and I think that’s what the public doesn’t understand,” said Chad Bown, from the Peterson Institute for International Economics and co-author with the journalist Soumaya Keynes of How to Win a Trade War, a book that will accompany many EU officials to the beach this summer.

He said it was not simply a question of “do we do tariffs or not?”, adding: “China is already doing all of these things and Europe has to respond. So then the question is, what’s the combination of policies?”

Plans are accelerating to exclude Chinese companies from procurement markets, to force investors in the electric vehicle supply chain to transfer technology and to compel European companies to diversify their supplier base to avoid over-concentration on Beijing.

Narrow product-based investigations into alleged Chinese dumping and subsidies are also gathering pace, and there are moves afoot to broaden those to subsectors using “safeguard” probes.

These would allow for emergency tariffs and quotas to be imposed on product groups, but would also target the whole world, rather than just Beijing.

For months, officials have been searching for chokepoints where China is dependent on Europe in an effort to “assess where we have leverage … we need to start trying to place ourselves in a situation where we can react as well”, according to a second senior official.

Meanwhile, Brussels is preparing for the expected Chinese blowback. Work is under way to establish a solidarity mechanism for companies caught in the crossfire by Chinese retaliation – something EU officials are certain will come.

The bloc has also been expanding its web of trading partners as it looks for alternative sources of mineral supplies following a spate of Chinese export controls – ranging from rare earths and graphite to gallium and germanium – but progress has been slow on stockpiling and building an indigenous supply chain in Europe.

Whether that is enough is debatable. But before assessing Europe’s chances, there is a more fundamental question about what victory would actually look like.

Nadine Godehardt, a specialist in China and geopolitics at the German Institute for International and Security Affairs, said “to some extent, we are in a trade war because everyone is positioning … both sides are more or less preparing”.

For Godehardt, victory would not be defeating China but surviving a decade of strategic competition while preserving enough of Europe’s industrial base, and ultimately its democratic model, to remain a viable geopolitical actor.

“The trade war is really not about winning in the sense that you can really gain something out of it. It’s a very long-term situation – more like the Hundred Years’ War … there’s lots of friction, and you have to be prepared for retaliation,” she said.

Virtually nobody believes the EU can win in an escalatory tit-for-tat conflict with Beijing. When Trump ramped up US tariffs on Chinese goods to 150 per cent last year, China forced an embarrassing climbdown by putting export controls on rare earth minerals and magnets. For Europe, this was a cautionary tale.

China knows Europe is a paper tiger … it won’t win a trade war

John Clarke , a former lead EU trade negotiator

“Realistically, will there be sort of a big bang type of assault? I’m not sure. The threat is so intense from China now, and they have made this so abundantly clear,” said Tobias Gehrke, a specialist in geoeconomics at the European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR), pointing to industrial supply chain security and outbound investment rules adopted by Beijing in recent months.

Instead, Europe’s best chance of survival may be through the plethora of smaller actions – described in an ECFR paper as a “distributed swarm” – each of which would bolster resilience or reduce dependence at a lower risk of a response such as cutting off rare earth supplies completely.

“This works like a military swarm … it is far harder for Beijing to swat away 50 small, dispersed measures at once than one large and threatening one,” wrote the think tank’s Asia chief Andrew Small.

Many of the elements of this “swarm” – from trade cases and supply chain mapping to diversification and leveraging the giant EU market – are already happening, with varying degrees of speed and success. There is a feeling, in Brussels at least, that Europe is more likely to succeed with this strategy.

“We don’t have this one tool that rules them all, but should unleash across the board all the measures, whether it’s cybersecurity, strict market access, public procurement or foreign subsidy regulation … all of them taken together can amount to something meaningful,” Gehrke said.

But the other big question is whether Europe has the stomach for a fight.

It is indisputable that the European Commission is getting its ducks in a row, but also hard to argue that the 27 member states have the requisite pain threshold.

Divisions among capitals have proven to be the EU’s Achilles’ heel in skirmishes against the US and China in recent years and as trade becomes increasingly securitised, national governments have more of a say than before.

“The competence of trade policy is moving to the member states … they are famously divided when it comes to how to deal with China,” said John Clarke, a former lead EU trade negotiator with a long history of dealing with Beijing.

“China knows that. They exploit it. They divide and rule.”

For this reason, perhaps, the EU’s most potent trade weapon, its anti-coercion instrument, has lain unused despite several high-profile attempts at economic bullying from both superpowers.

“I don’t think there’ll be a consensus inside the EU to have a direct fight with China. China knows Europe is a paper tiger … it won’t win a trade war,” Clarke said.

If Clarke is right, Europe is unlikely to emerge from a prolonged trade war with China as an outright victor – but it is unclear whether that should be the goal.

As Bown and Keynes argue in their book “a winner might be the one who is best able to preserve their economic model”, or simply “the team that loses less than a rival”.

By that measure, Europe may be content if it emerges from this most confrontational of eras with enough industry surviving to ensure political cohesion.

item.title: [SCMP] Hong Kong restaurants score extra HK$500 million thanks to World Cup
item.description: The World Cup brought in an extra HK$500 million (US$63.8 million) for Hong Kong’s catering sector this year, an industry leader has said, after restaurants and shopping centres hosted overnight viewings of the final with dim sum, snacks and milk tea. Businesses were also urged on Monday to devise creative strategies and set competitive prices to maintain the momentum from the city’s World Cup fever. The final between Spain and Argentina drew crowds of football fans, including children and the e
item.content:encoded:
The World Cup brought in an extra HK$500 million (US$63.8 million) for Hong Kong’s catering sector this year, an industry leader has said, after restaurants and shopping centres hosted overnight viewings of the final with dim sum, snacks and milk tea.

Businesses were also urged on Monday to devise creative strategies and set competitive prices to maintain the momentum from the city’s World Cup fever.

The final between Spain and Argentina drew crowds of football fans, including children and the elderly, who flocked to shopping centres at 3am – packing every floor of Tuen Mun Town Plaza, Olympian City, MCP in Tseung Kwan O and Kai Tak Mall.

Simon Wong Ka-wo, president of the Hong Kong Federation of Restaurants and Related Trades, estimated that the catering industry had pocketed an additional HK$500 million between June 30 and Monday, representing an uptick of about 6 per cent from its initial estimate in July.

In Tuen Mun Town Plaza, cheers erupted as Lionel Messi and Lamine Yamal appeared on a 540-inch screen, building to a crescendo when Spain clinched victory 1-0 after extra time.

Stalls and restaurants sought to capitalise on the crowds, setting up booths nearby to offer bubble tea or delivering dim sum directly to the viewing area.

Supermarket chain ParknShop stayed open overnight, selling drinks and fruit, while some spectators ordered food deliveries to the venue.

Many vendors offered promotions such as “buy three, get one free” to attract customers. Taxis also lined up at the mall’s entrance, ready to pick up passengers after the match.

Nini Man, manager of Nap Tea’s Tuen Mun branch, said the World Cup pop-up stand had only started turning a profit in the last two matches after the spot opened for a trial run on July 3.

She said she was inspired by the strong turnout at the mall for the Brazil-Japan match earlier in the tournament. As more vendors joined, the screenings evolved into a plaza-wide event.

“It was worth continuing [during the World Cup],” she said.

Man added that the exposure had helped introduce the shop to nearby residents who had never visited before.

“We have become like old neighbourhood friends,” she said, referring to the rare opportunity to interact with customers face-to-face.

Terence Chan Tak-ming, general manager of the portfolio leasing department at Henderson Land, said MCP recorded double-digit growth in both foot traffic and sales turnover throughout the World Cup.

He noted that tenants offering sports, lifestyle retail goods, or food and drinks had performed particularly well, creating business opportunities outside traditional peak hours.

In addition to vendors offering snacks and drinks ranging from coffee to craft beer, MCP also arranged free shuttle buses to Central and Mong Kok to help fans travel directly to work on Monday morning after watching the final.

Catering sector leader Wong said that while the significant time difference between Hong Kong and North America had dampened the usual business boost over the match period, many fans still visited restaurants and shopping centres to watch the final.

Venues raking in extra business included traditional Chinese dim sum restaurants.

“Restaurants need new directions in operations to attract new clientele. For important matches in the future, now that large screens have been installed, restaurants can roll out different activities and discounts to attract customers,” Wong said.

Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism Rosanna Law Shuk-pui, who watched the final at Kai Tak Mall, said that even with mega-events, businesses need to think creatively about strategies to attract customers.

She highlighted the emerging trend of traditional dim sum restaurants live-streaming World Cup matches as a successful example.

“I used to think watching a match in a dim sum restaurant might seem out of place, but it is actually feasible,” Law said. “That is why everyone has to rack their brains and examine their own strengths to discover more business opportunities.”

She added that a proactive approach from these businesses would help boost the economy.

Economist Simon Lee Siu-po said he believed business among restaurants and retailers would return to baseline levels after the World Cup, given lukewarm nighttime spending sentiment.

He urged the catering industry to be transparent and creative to attract patrons, citing the example of Lin Heung Lau’s Sheung Wan branch.

The restaurant rolled out an HK$3,888 eight-person VIP package and a HK$368 set for singles for the final match, with tea and service charges included.

Lee said the operator addressed the two pain points of Chinese restaurants by setting out their prices clearly and catering to the needs of solo diners.

“Hong Kong has too many options. With a competitive price and quality food, patrons will be interested,” he said, adding that restaurants could offer smaller meal portions to attract those dining alone.

Among those drawn to the city’s shopping malls for the final match’s “vibrant” atmosphere was Tuen Mun resident Shannon Dias, 42, who came to support Spain.

“A lot of people are here for both teams. It feels like a stadium – it’s better to be here,” he said.

Although he had eaten before the match, Dias said services by vendors at the shopping centre were “pretty good”.

“They’re doing their best to set up shop, especially for an event like this,” he said. “It definitely helps boost the economy.”

Another fan, Kit Ching, attended with his family after seeing promotions on social media.

Despite the crowds, he said he enjoyed the atmosphere and spent about HK$50 on drinks and snacks from ParknShop before kick-off.

“It was good to have something to eat while watching,” he said.

But some Argentina supporters left disappointed after the final whistle.

McQueen Zeng, who watched the match with friends, said it was a “pity” Argentina could not defend their title.

“I applaud Spain, and I also applaud our Argentinian players because they kept making efforts and trying to score,” he said. “I’m grateful to them for that.”

Zeng added that he would continue to support the team and hoped to watch a future World Cup in person, noting that Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay would co-host the opening three centenary matches for the 2030 Fifa tournament.

Additional reporting by Jess Ma

item.title: [SCMP] Malaysia aims for 600,000 South Korean arrivals in new travel drive
item.description: As Malaysia pushes to welcome 47 million international visitors under this year’s travel campaign, the country is setting its sights on bringing South Korean arrivals back above pre-pandemic levels by promoting authentic local experiences beyond its well-known beach resorts and city attractions. “South Korea is one of our priority markets,” Kamilia Hani Abdul Halim, director of Tourism Malaysia Seoul, said in an interview. Last year, about 558,000 South Koreans visited Malaysia, making it the co
item.content:encoded:
As Malaysia pushes to welcome 47 million international visitors under this year’s travel campaign, the country is setting its sights on bringing South Korean arrivals back above pre-pandemic levels by promoting authentic local experiences beyond its well-known beach resorts and city attractions.

“South Korea is one of our priority markets,” Kamilia Hani Abdul Halim, director of Tourism Malaysia Seoul, said in an interview.

Last year, about 558,000 South Koreans visited Malaysia, making it the country’s eighth-largest source market for international visitors. This year, Malaysia hopes to increase that figure to 600,000.

“Our arrivals from South Korea have yet to return to pre-pandemic levels,” Kamilia said. “Before Covid-19, we welcomed more than 600,000 South Korean visitors, so this is an important milestone for us.”

The tourism drive forms part of the government’s global Visit Malaysia 2026 campaign, which aims to attract 47 million international visitors despite growing uncertainties affecting the global travel industry.

At the heart of the campaign is the theme “Surreal Experiences” – reflecting a shift in travellers’ expectations following the pandemic.

“Nowadays, travellers are looking for authentic experiences,” the Malaysian tourism chief in South Korea said. “They don’t want crowded places any more. They want something new.”

To meet that demand, Malaysia has broadened its tourism offerings beyond traditional leisure travel, promoting nature, wellness, rural tourism, culture and heritage, gastronomy, sports and educational tourism.

The campaign also reflects changing travel patterns among South Koreans.

Kamilia said the way South Koreans travel had altered dramatically since the pandemic. South Korean tourists are becoming increasingly interested in exploring destinations at their own pace rather than following conventional itineraries like packaged tour programmes.

“In the past, many South Koreans came on package tours,” she said. “Now they prefer to explore on their own.”

Instead of relying on organised itineraries, travellers in the postpandemic era tend to book flights independently, navigate Malaysian cities using public transport and ride-hailing services and search online for unique local experiences.

“People who can speak English, or simply find information online, now arrange their own trips,” she said. “That’s why we are creating more themed packages and promoting activities that travellers may not have known about before.”

To reach these increasingly independent travellers, Tourism Malaysia has expanded its digital outreach through social media, South Korean influencers, bloggers and online platforms, while also partnering airlines including Malaysia Airlines, AirAsia, Batik Air and Jeju Air to promote direct routes to Kuala Lumpur and Kota Kinabalu.

New experiences

While golf holidays and resort holidays still remain popular among South Koreans, Tourism Malaysia is increasingly encouraging visitors to discover different sides of the country. “Now we’re promoting other experiences as well, such as educational tourism. Many South Korean families are sending their children to Malaysia to learn English.”

Besides widely known destinations among South Koreans such as Kuala Lumpur and the resort island of Langkawi, Tourism Malaysia is also highlighting lesser-known destinations – including Sarawak on the island of Borneo and the northern state of Perak.

Sarawak, famed for its ancient rainforests and diverse Indigenous communities, recently earned Unesco Global Geopark recognition for the Sarawak Delta Geopark. The destination could also become more accessible to Korean travellers should direct air links be established in the future.

“If you like nature, Sarawak is an ideal destination,” Kamilia said. “It is famous for its food as well, and we’re hoping to have direct flights from South Korea someday.”

Perak, meanwhile, offers a different appeal. “It is reachable by land from Kuala Lumpur, making it perfect for a day trip,” she said. “It’s a quieter destination, but it’s well known for its gastronomy and gives visitors a chance to experience another side of Malaysia.”

One area where Tourism Malaysia has also seen particularly strong momentum is gastronomy. South Korean interest in Malaysian cuisine has grown rapidly in recent years, helped by the opening of more Malaysian restaurants in Seoul and the increasing exposure of Malaysian dishes through Korean television travel programmes, according to Kamilia.

“We’ve noticed that many Koreans are becoming interested in Malaysian food,” she said. “When you watch South Korean TV programmes these days, many of them travel to Malaysia and introduce local dishes like roti canai and teh tarik.”

Food has therefore become one of Tourism Malaysia’s key promotional pillars at travel fairs and cultural festivals in Korea.

Durian diplomacy

One product receiving an especially warm response is Malaysia’s premium Musang King durian. “Many people think South Koreans don’t like durians because of the smell,” Kamilia said with a laugh. “But when we introduced Musang King here, we received a very good response.”

The agency has even begun promoting seasonal “durian tourism” packages that allow visitors to tour orchards during harvest season and sample freshly picked fruit. “You can find durian in chocolates, cakes, cookies and even McDonald’s serves a durian sundae during the season in Malaysia.”

Kamilia’s own connection to South Korea stretches back more than 15 years. Before joining Tourism Malaysia’s overseas network, she studied Korean language for a year at Seoul National University’s language institute.

Returning to Seoul as the head of Tourism Malaysia has allowed her to witness how much both countries’ understanding of one another has evolved.

“When I first studied here in 2010, many South Koreans didn’t really know Malaysia. People often asked whether I was from Indonesia,” she recalled. “Now it’s completely different. Many Koreans tell me they’ve visited Malaysia. They know nasi lemak, roti canai and our multicultural society. That has been a very pleasant surprise.”

She believes that background has also helped her better understand South Korean travellers and tailor Malaysia’s marketing strategy accordingly.

“It helps because I understand Korean culture and what Korean travellers are looking for,” she said. “If they have feedback or complaints, I can understand where they’re coming from and relay that to our headquarters.”

Tourism Malaysia plans to hold a Malaysia Fair at Dongdaemun Design Plaza in Seoul this November, featuring cultural performances and promotional events showcasing the country’s diverse heritage.

“We hope more South Koreans will come and discover that Malaysia offers much more than they may already know,” she said. “There are always new experiences waiting for them.”

This article was first published by The Korea Times

item.title: [SCMP] Bridging the divide: how AI scientists unite despite growing geopolitical rifts
item.description: For policymakers in Beijing and Washington, artificial intelligence (AI) has become a high-stakes arena in their strategic rivalry, closely tied to national interests and security. From export controls on high-end semiconductors to heavy scrutiny of foreign investment, the future of AI would appear to represent a stark binary choice: one country’s technological leap is seen as a direct threat to the other. Yet, far from the halls of power, some developers and scientists contend that the true pot
item.content:encoded:
For policymakers in Beijing and Washington, artificial intelligence (AI) has become a high-stakes arena in their strategic rivalry, closely tied to national interests and security.

From export controls on high-end semiconductors to heavy scrutiny of foreign investment, the future of AI would appear to represent a stark binary choice: one country’s technological leap is seen as a direct threat to the other.

Yet, far from the halls of power, some developers and scientists contend that the true potential of AI is in expanding global capabilities.

This view united a diverse group of AI innovators and entrepreneurs who gathered in Hong Kong this month to explore how global collaboration and knowledge sharing could forge an entrepreneurial ecosystem that transcends deepening geopolitical rifts.

For Pak-Sun Ting, co-founder and CEO of Votee AI – developer of the first open-source Cantonese large language model (LLM) – scientific progress and collaboration in AI are like a “small undercurrent to help go against geopolitics”.

“Geopolitical winds change from time to time,” Ting said. “Government to government, there could be some disagreements. But from civilian to civilian, technology to technology, as long as they allow us and it is doable, we will take it and work with it.”

Votee AI has built partnerships with universities in Hong Kong and the United States, and with Chinese tech giants. It has also set up an AI research lab in Toronto, as well as regional hubs in Kuala Lumpur and Ho Chi Minh City.

The company is on a mission to bring AI to underserved languages and replicate its foundry model to preserve languages in Southeast Asia, Africa and elsewhere.

“These collaborations validate the fact that geopolitics – if you take a big enough ruler, that is not going to matter much,” Ting said.

The former banking executive’s pragmatism is echoed by Votee AI’s technology adviser, John Whaley, who also serves as an adjunct lecturer in computer science at Stanford University and runs an accelerator for AI founders named Inception Studio.

Whaley said the commercial and productivity incentives for AI adoption were so immense that they pulled the global industry towards integration. “The size of the opportunity dwarfs even the governments and geopolitics. The amount of productivity boost and positive impact is much bigger than any individual country.”

“This is why everyone is trying to vie for who is going to be the leader in the AI world,” Whaley added, highlighting the focus on sovereign AI and the strategic importance of access to AI, data, and graphics processing unit (GPU) and computing power.

Global AI growth should not be a zero-sum game, he said, calling the creation of new AI technologies an expansion of the overall “pie” of global economic growth and productivity.

“A world where people have access, knowledge and capability – that is the world that we want to live in. It is going to help so much. There are a lot of things that humans should not be doing manually any more.”

Whaley also highlighted changes in the AI regulation landscape, saying the US government had been shifting from an unregulated stance on the matter to a “new era” in which certain models were flagged as national security risks or of strategic importance.

When it comes to oversight, some leading AI developers in the US have suggested an even more cautious approach.

For instance, Dario Amodei, CEO of American AI research company Anthropic, wrote last month that AI’s exponential growth necessitated government oversight.

Amodei called for legal frameworks that would grant governments the authority to “block or deter deployment of the model if it is determined … to present unacceptable risks”.

“US export controls on frontier chips and semiconductor manufacturing equipment to China have been a major contributor to the US’ overall lead in AI, and these policies need to be expanded, tightened and coordinated with other like-minded states,” he added.

Meanwhile, as innovators around the world work on AI, Whaley described the future of AI as global and “not going to be necessarily just centred in Silicon Valley”, where he said there was “a hyper concentration of capital and talent”.

The next great AI company could very well emerge from Hong Kong or the Greater Bay Area, he added.

Whaley voiced an eagerness to bring the founder and builder energy to the region, as his accelerator organises intense retreats for select AI founders to workshop their business ideas and pitch to potential investors.

“Silicon Valley is Silicon Valley because it is a place where [failure] is very socially accepted. People happily, proudly tell you about all the things that they tried and failed. Culturally, it may be a little bit harder to do that here in Hong Kong.”

He said the focus should be on fostering an environment with freedom to experiment and build, rather than replicating the Silicon Valley model and overlooking the robust local university system and available capital.

Chinat Yu, an AI researcher and strategist from Hong Kong, is working to bridge that gap, having graduated from Stanford’s master’s programme in learning, design and technology in 2024.

By leveraging Hong Kong’s status as a regional “human capital magnet”, the city could synthesise local research with an entrepreneurial spirit that encourages STEM students to pursue moonshot innovations, Yu said.

“Too many people ask if it is the US or China [that will win the AI race],” said Yu, who helped organise this month’s Hong Kong AI Summit. “The right question is to ask how you do it [with both] the US and China.”

“By building this bridge between the two bay areas and connecting those innovation hubs, we are able to hopefully transform the culture here such that this then becomes the future innovation hub globally.”

“A lot of companies from China that are thinking about going global are already naturally going through Hong Kong,” he added, pointing to Chinese AI start-ups like Zhipu and MiniMax going public and being listed in the city.

Yu said that by bringing in mentors who had been in the start-up trenches for decades, local founders and aspiring entrepreneurs could learn to define customer needs and problem-solve as innovators.

“We want to build local but dream global.”

And as AI collapses the barriers to building software, the pool of the individual creator who can take part in developing new technologies is broadening.

Quincy Larson, founder of online learning platform freeCodeCamp.org, said the focus for acquiring programming skills should not be rote syntax, but rather domain-specific expertise fused with technical literacy.

“I would encourage people to learn the fundamentals of programming, but you do not need to spend thousands of hours getting good at programming any more, unless you really want to focus on some area that is beyond what LLMs are capable of.”

Larson envisioned a future in which the next wave of builders would be industry experts – lawyers, accountants and managers of different fields.

“Everybody is going to need to become a technical product manager,” he said. “They need to understand the fundamentals. They also need to understand some domain that they have a lot of insight into.”

“They understand that world really well and now they can potentially build solutions within their existing organisation or create a company that builds those solutions.”

Larson said that as his platform provided children in California and western China access to the same learning resources for free, the future was “more about one person who holds all the moving parts in their head and shared memories in the computer”.
